content.text: Structured logs from a GenAI service contain sensitive metadata even after PII scrubbing: which users trigger which features, how often, at what cost, and with what error patterns. Log access control defines
— and audit logging ensures that every time someone queries sensitive log data, that access is itself recorded.

This topic covers the two-tenant log access model (operational vs prompt-audit), Loki and Grafana RBAC configuration, how to emit and store access audit events, and the quarterly access review process.

attrs.alt: Two-tenant Loki access model with RBAC roles and access audit trail

content.text: All GenAI log data lives in one of two Loki tenants:
Metadata events (latency, errors, costs, token counts) — no prompt/completion text
Prompt and completion text — only when explicit user consent is collected
separation is enforced at the network layer
, not just by application config. The prompt-audit Loki instance runs on a separate hostname with a separate access policy. Even an
who has full access to the operational tenant cannot query the prompt-audit tenant without a named role assignment and a valid approval ticket.
header to route requests to a specific tenant. Each Grafana data source is configured with a fixed
value, so the tenant is determined by which datasource the engineer uses — not by a query parameter they can change.

# grafana/datasources/loki-operational.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Loki (Operational)
    type: loki
    url: https://loki.internal.example.com
    jsonData:
      httpHeaderName1: X-Scope-OrgID
    secureJsonData:
      httpHeaderValue1: operational   # hardcoded — user cannot override

  - name: Loki (Prompt Audit)
    type: loki
    url: https://loki-audit.internal.example.com   # separate instance
    jsonData:
      httpHeaderName1: X-Scope-OrgID
    secureJsonData:
      httpHeaderValue1: prompt-audit
    # This datasource is NOT visible to most users — controlled by Grafana team RBAC
Engineers can only query the Loki (Prompt Audit) datasource if Grafana's data source permissions grant their team access to it. Data source permissions are configured per role:
Within Grafana, roles map to what each team can see and do across both log tenants. The role matrix:
In Grafana Enterprise, define these as custom RBAC roles. In open-source Grafana, use

# grafana/provisioning/access-control/roles.yaml (Grafana Enterprise)
apiVersion: 1
roles:
  - name: GenAI On-Call
    description: Read-only access to operational logs for own team during on-call
    global: false
    permissions:
      - action: datasources:query
        scope: datasources:name:Loki (Operational)
      - action: dashboards:read
        scope: folders:name:GenAI Operational
      # Explicitly denied — cannot be granted even if requested
      - action: datasources:query
        scope: datasources:name:Loki (Prompt Audit)

  - name: Prompt Audit Reviewer
    description: Access to prompt audit logs for named safety reviewers
    global: false
    permissions:
      - action: datasources:query
        scope: datasources:name:Loki (Prompt Audit)
Accessing logs is itself an action that must be logged. This is the
: every query against the prompt-audit tenant (and high-sensitivity operational queries) writes an audit event to a separate, tamper-resistant store.
Loki supports audit logging via a middleware layer that intercepts API requests before they reach the query engine:

import time, hashlib, json, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional
from fastapi import Request, Response
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware

audit_log = logging.getLogger("log-access-audit")

@dataclass
class LogAccessEvent:
    event: str = "log.access"
    accessor_id: str = ""          # email or service account, hashed for privacy in the audit log itself
    accessor_email_hash: str = ""
    tenant: str = ""               # operational | prompt-audit
    query: str = ""                # the LogQL query (truncated to 512 chars)
    time_range_start: str = ""
    time_range_end: str = ""
    rows_returned: int = 0
    duration_ms: int = 0
    purpose: Optional[str] = None          # required for prompt-audit tenant
    approval_ticket: Optional[str] = None  # required for prompt-audit tenant
    ts_epoch_ms: int = 0

class LogAccessAuditMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    PROMPT_AUDIT_TENANT = "prompt-audit"

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next) -> Response:
        start_ms = int(time.time() * 1000)
        tenant = request.headers.get("X-Scope-OrgID", "unknown")

        response = await call_next(request)

        if tenant == self.PROMPT_AUDIT_TENANT or request.url.path.startswith("/loki/api"):
            body_bytes = b""
            async for chunk in response.body_iterator:
                body_bytes += chunk

            rows = 0
            try:
                data = json.loads(body_bytes)
                rows = sum(len(r.get("values", [])) for r in data.get("data", {}).get("result", []))
            except Exception:
                pass

            user_email = request.headers.get("X-Grafana-User", "unknown")
            evt = LogAccessEvent(
                accessor_id=hashlib.sha256(user_email.encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
                accessor_email_hash=hashlib.sha256(user_email.encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
                tenant=tenant,
                query=str(request.query_params.get("query", ""))[:512],
                time_range_start=request.query_params.get("start", ""),
                time_range_end=request.query_params.get("end", ""),
                rows_returned=rows,
                duration_ms=int(time.time() * 1000) - start_ms,
                purpose=request.headers.get("X-Audit-Purpose"),
                approval_ticket=request.headers.get("X-Audit-Ticket"),
                ts_epoch_ms=start_ms,
            )
            # Write to a separate, append-only audit store
            audit_log.info(json.dumps(asdict(evt)))

            from starlette.responses import Response as StarletteResponse
            return StarletteResponse(
                content=body_bytes,
                status_code=response.status_code,
                headers=dict(response.headers),
                media_type=response.media_type,
            )
        return response
Enforcing Purpose and Approval for Prompt-Audit Access
Every query to the prompt-audit tenant must carry two additional headers:
: a free-text description of why the data is needed (e.g.,
: a ticket number from the team's issue tracker, which must be approved by a manager before access is granted
If either header is missing, the Loki proxy rejects the request with HTTP 403:

from fastapi import Request, HTTPException

async def require_audit_headers(request: Request):
    tenant = request.headers.get("X-Scope-OrgID", "")
    if tenant == "prompt-audit":
        purpose = request.headers.get("X-Audit-Purpose", "").strip()
        ticket = request.headers.get("X-Audit-Ticket", "").strip()
        if not purpose:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=403,
                detail="X-Audit-Purpose header required for prompt-audit tenant access"
            )
        if not ticket:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=403,
                detail="X-Audit-Ticket header required — provide an approved ticket number"
            )
        # Optional: validate ticket exists and is approved via JIRA/Linear API
        # validate_ticket(ticket)  # raises 403 if not approved
Safety reviewers and on-call engineers need
that expires automatically. This is safer than granting a permanent role that someone forgets to revoke.
The pattern uses short-lived Grafana service account tokens:

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

GRAFANA_URL = "https://grafana.example.com"
ADMIN_TOKEN = "..."

def grant_temporary_prompt_audit_access(
    engineer_email: str,
    approval_ticket: str,
    duration_hours: int = 48,
) -> dict:
    '''Create a time-limited service account token for prompt-audit access.'''
    # 1. Create a service account for this access grant
    sa = requests.post(
        f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/serviceaccounts",
        json={
            "name": f"temp-audit-{approval_ticket}",
            "role": "Viewer",   # minimal base role
        },
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {ADMIN_TOKEN}"},
    ).json()

    # 2. Assign the Prompt Audit Reviewer role to this service account
    requests.post(
        f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/access-control/users/{sa['id']}/roles",
        json={"roleUid": "prompt-audit-reviewer-role-uid"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {ADMIN_TOKEN}"},
    )

    # 3. Create a token with a hard expiry
    expiry = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(hours=duration_hours)
    token = requests.post(
        f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/serviceaccounts/{sa['id']}/tokens",
        json={
            "name": f"audit-{approval_ticket}",
            "secondsToLive": duration_hours * 3600,
        },
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {ADMIN_TOKEN}"},
    ).json()

    # 4. Log the grant itself as an audit event
    audit_log.info(json.dumps({
        "event": "log.access.grant",
        "grantee_email_hash": hashlib.sha256(engineer_email.encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
        "approval_ticket": approval_ticket,
        "expires_at": expiry.isoformat(),
        "tenant": "prompt-audit",
    }))

    return {
        "token": token["key"],   # send to the engineer via approved channel
        "expires_at": expiry.isoformat(),
    }
Access rights accumulate over time. The
is a mandatory process where each team lead confirms who in their team still needs access to production logs. Access that cannot be justified is revoked.
The access audit trail enables alerting on suspicious access patterns:
Single user queries > 50 000 rows in 1 hour
prompt-audit query outside 06:00–22:00 local time without an active incident
X-Audit-Ticket missing on prompt-audit query (bypassed middleware)
Same token used from two different IPs within 10 minutes
403 response on prompt-audit query by non-reviewer

# alerts/log-access-anomaly.yaml
groups:
  - name: log_access_anomalies
    rules:
      - alert: BulkLogExport
        expr: |
          sum by (accessor_id) (
            increase(log_access_rows_returned_total{tenant="prompt-audit"}[1h])
          ) > 50000
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: observability-leads
        annotations:
          summary: 'Bulk prompt-audit log export: {{ $labels.accessor_id }} queried {{ $value | humanize }} rows in 1h'

      - alert: PromptAuditAccessWithoutTicket
        expr: |
          count(
            log_access_events_total{
              tenant="prompt-audit",
              approval_ticket=""
            }
          ) > 0
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: security
        annotations:
          summary: 'Prompt-audit access without approval ticket — possible middleware bypass'
Access control is only effective if engineers understand it. Every new engineer must complete an
Read the log access policy (this document + PII scrubbing standards)
Complete the data handling training module (covers GDPR, PCI DSS, prompt data sensitivity)
Receive team-appropriate access (feature-team role for their team's staging/dev streams)
Understand that production log access requires active on-call assignment, not a standing grant
Understand that prompt-audit access requires a manager-approved ticket for every access session

: engineers should have exactly the log access needed to do their job today, not the maximum access that might be useful someday. Access expands on demand via the ticket process and contracts automatically via quarterly review.
Log access controls for GenAI services enforce two independent access boundaries: operational logs (metadata only) and prompt-audit logs (sensitive content with explicit consent).
Two Loki tenants with separate hostnames prevent cross-tenant access even for admins
Grafana RBAC restricts which datasource each team can query — engineers cannot switch tenants by changing a header
Every query to the prompt-audit tenant requires X-Audit-Purpose and X-Audit-Ticket headers; the middleware logs the access event
Time-limited tokens (48h) are granted per approval ticket for safety reviewers — no standing access
A quarterly access review removes stale grants; a nightly script identifies users with no logins in 90+ days
Anomalous access patterns (bulk exports, off-hours prompt queries, missing tickets) trigger security alerts
